← the Atlas

Remote terminals from scratch — host-as-authority, vertical slices

feature · seedling ·proposed ·JUN 19, 2026

If we threw away PR

Contents

  • The core model
  • What we stand on, what we throw away
    • Stand on (the shipped foundation is correct)
    • Throw away (this is the #1385 design being rejected)
  • Ten risk-isolated slices
    • Honest cost scorecard
  • The four bug classes, dissolved by construction
  • The honest correction: awareness is split
  • One contract, two wirings — compose (local) vs serve+mirror (ssh)
  • Versus the resequencing
  • Status

Assume we throw away both #1385 and its design, and start from the goal. One way to ship it is a resequencing of #1385 — reverse-engineering safe horizontal layers out of the code we already have, which keeps the watcher in the PTY path, keeps hostId-absent-means-local threaded through the contract, keeps persisted remote-terminal records, and merely re-times the cold-boot orphan reconcile (the fbc06c31 10-second settle band-aid). This note does the opposite: it designs vertically and risk-first against a cleaner core, and instead of re-timing the boot race it removes the race’s inputs. It stands on the shipped substrate (kaval, getHostSession, the TerminalEndpoint seam — all merged and validated) and rebuilds only the part #1385 got wrong: how kolu-the-app consumes that substrate.

The core model

One thesis, and everything follows: the host is the sole source of truth for its own live terminals, and kolu persists only a dial-list — the set of hostIds to re-dial, never a remote-terminal record. A remote tile is just a local TerminalEndpoint (the shipped PTY·fs·git seam) whose surfaces are bound to an ssh transport; local vs remote is only the transport.

The host agent runs where the files live, and only the wiring varies. Locally the providers compose directly into kolu-server’s surface — a { collections, procedures } + deps fragment spread in, exactly how @kolu/surface-app already composes into kolu-server (one router, no client, no wire, no Nix). The diagram shows the remote topology; remotely the host agent serves the same surface fragment over stdioLink and kolu-server mirrors it. Same contract, same provider code — only compose-vs-serve+mirror changes.

The load-bearing principles:

  • Existence comes from one atomic pull, never a stream. Boot, restore, and reconnect collapse into one routine: dial the host, await a single complete kaval terminal.list, synchronously seed a terminalMetadata entry (cwd + location) for every listed id so the client’s membership filter (meta.keys()) reaches its full set in one tick, then project. There is no shadow tile and no empty→partial→full enumeration for the boot race to mis-settle on.
  • Persist only a dial-list. Persist a record only for an endpoint that dies with you (the local PTY). The durable remote kaval outlives you, so you persist only Set<hostId> to re-dial — and that dial-list is the sole boot-dial authority. A remote-located terminal can never enter SavedSession.terminals (unit-tested), so no autosave can ever prune it.
  • Endpoint is first-class. location is a non-optional discriminated union — { kind: "local" } or { kind: "remote", hostId } — so local is an explicit variant, not a reserved host-id string (a remote host aliased local in ~/.ssh/config can’t collide with the in-process endpoint). A dropped location is a compile error, not a silent local respawn. (Shipped — #1402 .)
  • Awareness is split by where its inputs live — the one place a naïve clean-slate (and #1385’s own framing) goes wrong. See below.

What we stand on, what we throw away

Stand on (the shipped foundation is correct)

  • The TerminalEndpoint PTY·fs·git seam — the one correct shape; local and remote are the same interface over different transports.
  • getHostSession / HostSession — the genuinely hard, already-solved physics: ssh reach + Nix provision + the ref-counted reconnect/give-up machine. The host-as-truth model leans on its reconnect loop to re-pull terminal.list.
  • kaval + its atomic terminal.list — a durable remote PTY with zero kolu logic on the host, plus the complete-list pull that is the real canvas-existence signal.
  • The synchronous seedMeta upsert at spawn — the client paints a tile only when its metadata has arrived, so this is kept and generalized to seed every id from the list.

Throw away (this is the #1385 design being rejected)

  • The watcher forwarding the PTY. reject Putting the PTY data path through the most-volatile host process. Dial kaval directly for the PTY; the host agent keeps the PTY out of its path.
  • Persisted remote-terminal records + the dual boot-dial authority. reject Replaced by the dial-list, which supersedes the savedRemoteTerminals → bootDialHosts branch.
  • preRegisterSavedTerminals → shadow tiles + the settle-window orphan reconcile fbc06c3 . reject Deleted, not re-timed — replaced by the one atomic terminal.list + synchronous seed.
  • Optional hostId (“absent ⇒ local”) threaded through the contract. reject Replaced by the non-optional location sum on the terminal surfaces — {kind:"local"} ∣ {kind:"remote",hostId} (P1, shipped #1402 ); the activity feed’s hostId is finished off in P2b.

Ten risk-isolated slices

Each slice isolates one risk. The local-only bug-proofing lands first; then P4w is the lean local refactor — extract the provider pipeline into @kolu/terminal-providers as a surface fragment and compose it directly into kolu-server’s surface (the surface-app pattern — no client, no mirror, no wire) — and P4d is the fat remote phase: it adds the bin/serveOverStdio ssh entrypoint, the cross-arch Nix drv-map/closure-test (a kaval clone), provisioning, and serves the same fragment over stdioLink + mirrors it. Locally there’s no link at all (composition); the wire — and its mirror — appears only remotely. The long pole is P1 → P2 → P4a → P3 → P4d → P9.

PhaseKindCostShipsRetires the risk of…
P1 shipped #1402 refactorthinlocation is now a non-optional discriminated union ({kind:"local"} ∣ {kind:"remote",hostId}) — collision-proof vs a reserved local string; migrates pre-1.26 saved terminals; the seam createMetadata(cwd, location) makes a dropped location a compile error at every spawn siterestore-to-wrong-endpoint — a dropped host is a compile error, not a silent local respawn
P2bug-proofthina Set<hostId> dial-list as the sole boot-dial authority; autosave excludes remote terminals (tested); neuter the client respawn-by-hostId branchautosave-prunes + boot-stops-re-dialing — structurally unreachable
P2bbug-proofthinRecentRepo/RecentAgent hostId non-optional; prune-stat via endpointFor(record.hostId).fs, tolerant of a down hostlocal-fs assumptions reaching remote records
P4afeaturemoderatea kaval-direct durable remote shell over real ssh+Nix, PTY-tap awareness + the host login PATH; fs/git + git/agent show a typed “not yet”the walking skeleton — a durable remote shell with no host agent
P4wrefactormoderatethe lean local refactor: extract the in-server provider pipeline (terminalEndpoint/providers.ts) into @kolu/terminal-providers as a surface fragment — a { collections, procedures } contract + a watcherDeps impl bundle — and compose it into kolu-server’s surface (spread into the existing implementSurfaces call, exactly how @kolu/surface-app already composes in). No client, no mirror, no directLink, no bin/serveOverStdio, no Nix (all P4d). Providers publish awareness through the shared ctx; a thin in-server fold writes terminalMetadata via the existing updateServerMetadata (the persisted-vs-live fence survives). Behavior-preserving.the fictional in-process boundary — composing kills the mirror/fold/directLink and the lost-update window they dragged in
P3refactormoderateadoptHost: dial → await kaval’s atomic terminal.list → seed → project; client isLoading waits per host; delete the settle windowthe cold-boot re-adoption race — existence is kaval’s atomic list, never the watcher’s incremental mirror
P4dfeaturefatgo remote (the fat phase): add bin.ts + serveOverStdio + the --stdio entrypoint + captureLoginPath, the Nix derivation + closure-test + cross-arch drv-map (a clone of kaval’s shipped kavalBuildId/kavalDrvBySystem pattern), provisioning (nix copy→realise→pin via getHostSession), and serve the same surface fragment over stdioLink + mirror it in the remote endpoint (the mirror/fold lives only here, where there’s a real wire). Remote tiles get git/PR/agent badges and the Code tab in onethe cross-arch Nix closure + the genuinely-remote surface only a real box exercises: cross-arch .drv, ssh reconnect/recheck/ControlMaster, nix copy over ssh-ng
P5featuremoderateper-host health chip + cold-dial progress, with a pinned onState → ENDPOINT_STATES tablea minute-long dial looking hung; the lossy state projection
P6featurethinthe palette lists ~/.ssh/config ∪ KOLU_HOSTS_JSON hosts; pick one or type a targetretyping ssh targets — list every host, dial on demand
P9featuremoderatemany tiles/host + multiple hosts on one ssh ControlMaster; graceful degradation; lazy per-binary provisioningdoes the pooled link scale, and does one dead host degrade instead of stall?

Honest cost scorecard

Calling every slice “thin” was wrong. The honest split:

  • Cheap, regardless of which remote path ships — P1 · P2 · P2b (+ P6). Pure local-world refactors/UX; do them first — they turn the #1385 bug classes into compile errors and failing tests with no remote code.
  • Moderate — P4a, P4w, P3, P5, P9. Real but contained. P4w is the local consumption refactor — extract @kolu/terminal-providers as a surface fragment and compose it into kolu-server’s surface (no client, no mirror, no directLink, no ssh, no Nix); composition is what dissolves the mirror/fold the in-process boundary would otherwise have needed.
  • Fat — the cost centre — P4d. Go remote: the bin/serveOverStdio ssh entrypoint + captureLoginPath, the Nix closure (real deps @kolu/terminal-providers, kolu-git, gh, node, guarded by a watcher buildId.closure.test.ts), the cross-arch drv-map, provisioning, and serving the fragment over stdioLink + the remote mirror/fold. Two honest qualifiers: the Nix half is not novel/scary — it’s a near-verbatim clone of the shipped, tested kaval packaging (same shared pnpmDeps FOD, same hashString staleKey, a closure-test that is the analog of kaval’s); and the mirror/fold that P4w’s composition avoids genuinely reappears here — because remote is the only place there’s a real wire to mirror.

Caveat: P1 is now on master ( #1402 ) — landed directly on trunk as the recommended first move, not cherry-picked from #1385, and with a better shape than this note first proposed (a discriminated union, not a reserved LOCAL_HOST_ID string). P2–P4d are still ahead and map to commits on the open #1385 , so “merges independently and stays green” is a per-slice goal to re-validate for those.

The four bug classes, dissolved by construction

The point of designing afresh is that #1385’s bugs become unreachable, not fixed:

#1385 bug classThe construction that makes it impossible
Cold-boot re-adoption race (shadows wiped on an empty/partial frame; autosave prunes the session; boot stops re-dialing)Existence comes from kaval’s atomic terminal.list (dialed directly), never the watcher’s incremental mirror — so there’s no empty→partial→full enumeration to mis-settle, and the watcher only enriches existing keys. kolu persists only a dial-list (no record to prune); the client gate waits per host so a remote-only boot shows “dialing”, never a blank canvas. (#1385’s settle window existed precisely because it routed existence through the watcher’s incremental enumeration — this design removes that cause, it doesn’t re-time it.)
Env leak (remote PTY gets a restricted PATH)Spawn is {argv,env,initFiles} fully-specified; env is built fresh from system.info — and the host login PATH is captured host-side (moved into kaval’s system.info), so a kaval-direct dial reports it. An e2e asserts the PTY’s PATH carries the user’s profile dirs.
Remote-repo prune (recent-repos stat’d against local fs)Reachability is an endpoint question: every record carries its hostId, and prune-stat routes through endpointFor(record.hostId).fs, tolerant of a down host. A unit test asserts a remote record is never stat’d against local fs.
Restore-to-wrong-endpoint (saved hostId dropped → respawn locally)location is non-optional (a drop is a compile error); restore is dial + adopt the host’s list, never read-record-and-spawn; the client respawn-by-hostId branch is neutered.

The honest correction: awareness is split

The seductive clean-slate idea — and the one the adversarial review killed — was “the first remote slice needs no host agent at all, and the providers just run in kolu-server over kaval’s taps.” Half true, and the half that’s false is exactly the half #1385’s existence already proves.

Why git/PR/agent awareness MUST run host-side

The providers split cleanly by where its inputs live:

  • PTY-tap signals — cwd, title, foreground, command — ride kaval’s wire taps. These can run in kolu-server, and the kaval-direct shell slice (P4a) ships them with no host agent. That is a genuine surface-sharing win.
  • fs-derived signals — git branch/dirty state, PR status, agent detection — read the host’s own filesystem and home dir (git/resolve.ts runs simpleGit on the terminal’s cwd; the claude/codex/opencode detectors read ~/.claude under os.homedir()). Run in kolu-server against a remote cwd/pid, they inspect the wrong machine — empty git state, no PR, no agent, or a collision where a remote pid matches a local session file.

So git/PR/agent awareness must run on the host (P4w, the host agent) — which is exactly why kolu-watcher exists. The fresh design keeps that role and renames it the host agent, dropping only its PTY-forwarding. The honest scope: the host agent is the awareness host and the fs/git host, never the PTY path and never the canvas-existence signal. A from-scratch design that skipped this would re-learn #1385’s lesson the hard way.

One contract, two wirings — compose (local) vs serve+mirror (ssh)

The awareness providers are a surface — a typed contract — but in the same process you don’t dial a surface, you compose it. @kolu/surface supports this directly, and @kolu/surface-app already does it against kolu-server (surfaceAppSurfaceWith + surfaceAppServer): a sub-surface ships as two spreadable fragments — a contract fragment ({ collections, procedures }, spread into the parent defineSurface) and a deps fragment, spread into the parent’s implementSurfaces. One router, one transport, no client.

So terminal-providers does the same, and the only thing that varies is the wiring:

local   →  compose: spread { collections, procedures } + watcherDeps into kolu-server's surface   // one router · no wire · no client · no mirror
remote  →  serve the SAME fragment over stdioLink on the host; kolu-server mirrors it              // a real wire — the mirror lives here
  • P4w composes. The providers run in-server and publish their awareness collections through kolu-server’s own ctx; a thin in-server fold writes terminalMetadata via the existing updateServerMetadata. There is no directLink, no client, no mirror, no fold — and so no lost-update window and no firstPersisted/seed reconcile: those only existed because a stream hop sat between the providers and the metadata, and composition removes the hop (the providers enrich the record in place, like master — restored adoption state isn’t clobbered). The persisted-vs-live terminals:dirty fence is preserved (it lives in which mutator the fold calls). One carried-over detail: collections have no connect hook, so the providers’ publish binds to the parent ctx via the existing surfaceCtx late-bind holder (the same #1005 cycle-break kolu already uses) — kept kolu-local, so no drishti PR.
  • P4d serves + mirrors. The same contract fragment is wrapped in its own served router over stdioLink on the host (the bin/serveOverStdio half); kolu-server’s remote endpoint holds the client and mirrors + folds it. The mirror/fold — and its lost-update concern, now real over an RPC-wide wire — lives only here, alongside the genuinely-remote surface a real box exercises: cross-arch .drv, ssh reconnect/recheck/ControlMaster, nix copy over ssh-ng.

This is the honest version of “local vs remote is only the transport”: same contract fragment, same provider code — locally it’s composed (no link at all), remotely it’s served + mirrored. The boundary is real only where the processes actually are. (The earlier draft routed the local case through directLink too — a fictional in-process boundary that dragged in the mirror/fold/lost-update machinery; composing dissolves all of it.)

Versus the resequencing

A layered resequencing of #1385’s existing code and this from-scratch design are two real options, and they differ in kind:

  • Resequencing (finish #1385’s design in safe slices) is layer-completion of the existing design: lower review risk, faster to start (the code exists), but it preserves the worst coupling — the watcher in the PTY path, persisted remote records, the settle-window band-aid, optional-hostId everywhere. You ship the same architecture in safe slices.
  • from-scratch is dissolution-by-construction on a cleaner core: more up-front rewrite, but the four bug classes become unreachable, the PTY is dialed straight at kaval, and the boot model is one atomic pull. Every remote PR is demoable from the first one, and the load-bearing awareness split is isolated and honestly scoped.

A pragmatic blend is available: the structural bug-proofing here (P1/P2/P2b — endpoint-first-class, dial-list, host-aware feed) is cheap, local-only, and worth doing regardless of which path ships the remote code — they convert the bug classes into compile errors and failing tests on the world we already have. P4w earns its place in that set too: extracting the provider pipeline into @kolu/terminal-providers and composing it as a surface fragment is better modularity and testability on its own — a good refactor even if remote never ships (the composed surface is also the seam that lets remote reuse the same contract).

Status

Proposed — a clean-slate alternative to finishing #1385 in layers, and a child of kaval-sessions. This design was built from the goal, grounded in the shipped foundation by reading the real repo, judged by a panel, and adversarially verified — the verification forced the awareness split (the draft’s “no host agent, full awareness” thesis was provably wrong) and the seed-from-kaval’s-terminal.list existence model; a later pass found the sharper consumption model: the providers compose directly into kolu-server’s surface for LOCAL terminals (P4w — extract @kolu/terminal-providers as a surface fragment and spread it in, the way @kolu/surface-app already composes — no client, no mirror, no wire), and going remote (P4d — the fat phase: bin/serveOverStdio + the cross-arch Nix drv-map/closure-test (a kaval clone) + provisioning) serves the same fragment over stdioLink and mirrors it, so the wire — and its mirror — exists only where the processes are. The long pole is P1 → P2 → P4a → P3 → P4d → P9; the lean local watcher refactor (P4w) rides parallel to P4a, and the fat remote phase is P4d. It is not committed — and P1–P4d map to commits on the still-open #1385 , not merged trunk, so “merges independently / stays green” is a per-slice goal to re-validate. The structural trio P1/P2/P2b is the recommended first move either way — pure local-world refactors that make the #1385 bug classes unreachable before any remote code is written. P1 has landed on master ( #1402 ), in a better shape than first proposed: location is a discriminated union ({kind:"local"} ∣ {kind:"remote",hostId}), so a remote host aliased local can never collide with the in-process endpoint. Next: P2 (dial-list) and P2b (host-aware feed).

← the Atlas github ↗
